meta.title: The Future of Agentic Web Design — Workhorse × GoDoGood

meta.ogTitle: The Future of Web Design Is Agentic
meta.ogDescription: How AI agents are reshaping how we build, maintain, and sell websites — and what it means for the design industry.

header.title: The Future of Web Design Is Agentic
header.subtitle: A conversation between TJ Cichecki (Workhorse) and Rob Sulzer (GoDoGood) on how AI agents are reshaping how we build, maintain, and sell websites — and what it means for the design industry.

premise.label: The Premise
premise.heading: The website industry is at an inflection point
paragraphs: For two decades, the cycle has been the same: a client needs a website, a team spends months designing and developing it in WordPress, and three years later everyone does it again. The tools have gotten better, but the fundamental model — designers hand off to developers, developers grind through code, clients wait — hasn't changed.

Until now. The emergence of AI coding agents (like Claude Code) combined with headless CMS platforms (like Sanity) is collapsing the timeline from months to days, while actually improving the quality of the output. This isn't a theoretical future. It's happening right now.

quote.text: We're not waiting on people coding. We're not. That's the elephant in the room.

cards.title: Speed, Not Price
cards.description: Clients aren't asking for cheaper websites. They're asking for faster ones. The bottleneck has always been development — waiting on code, waiting on revisions, waiting on QA. AI agents eliminate that bottleneck entirely.

cards.title: Open Source Ownership
cards.description: Clients — especially nonprofits — want to own their digital assets. Not rent them on Webflow. Not be locked into a proprietary platform. The new stack (Next.js + headless CMS) gives them a real, portable asset on their balance sheet.

cards.title: Design Is the Differentiator
cards.description: When anyone can generate code, the thing that separates great from generic is the design eye, the strategy, and the brand thinking. The purple-gradient AI aesthetic is already exhausting people. Taste wins.

cards.title: The Designer Becomes the Operator
cards.description: Instead of directing interns or managing developer handoffs, the designer orchestrates AI agents — assigning tasks, reviewing output, refining direction. Same creative leadership, radically different execution.

cards.title: Authenticity Is Coming Back
cards.description: Clients can already spot AI-generated content. The brands that win will be the ones that feel human, intentional, and authentic — which requires real strategic thinking, not just prompting.

cards.title: The Market Is Massive
cards.description: There are roughly 100,000+ qualifying nonprofits in the US alone, and at any given time 20% are actively looking to redo their website. That's 20,000 potential clients — many of whom are underserved by the current agency model.

items.text: Not even cheaper. They just want it done quicker. And I need it to look gorgeous. I need the strategy.
The most qualified to make a website for a company is probably the company themselves. They know the subject matter. They're the better experts in their own work. They just don't know the technology.

You're going to coordinate agents that are going to do this for you. That's going to save your career as a creative director, as a strategist. Instead of directing interns, you're directing agents within these platforms.
This took me like a day and a half to build. Total hours combined, probably like 10 hours. And most of it wasn't actually working Claude Code — it was the conversations, the strategy.
items.attribution: TJ Cichecki, on building a complete portfolio site with CMS

items.text: Whoever has the brand is going to win. Authenticity, the experience with your product or service, the look, the feel — that's what's going to survive.

process.label: The Process
process.heading: How agentic web design works
process.intro: This is the emerging workflow — a designer-led, AI-powered process that collapses the traditional agency timeline while keeping strategy and brand at the center.

steps.title: Discovery & Kickoff
steps.description: One 45-minute meeting with the client. Gather the brand story, goals, audience, tone, and visual direction. This is where the human insight happens — understanding what the client actually needs versus what they think they need.

steps.title: AI-Assisted Research & Direction
steps.description: Use AI tools (ChatGPT for planning, Claude for execution) to research visual direction, generate mood concepts, and draft content from the conversation. Feed style references and brand guidelines as prompts. This replaces weeks of mood boarding with hours of iteration.

steps.title: Design Concept & Client Review
steps.description: Present a design direction based on the research. This can be a single well-designed page — the AI agents will extrapolate the system from there. One more 45-minute meeting to align on the look and feel.

steps.title: Agentic Development
steps.description: Deploy Claude Code to build the full site — front-end pages, responsive design, animations, and a complete headless CMS backend (Sanity). The AI builds the content management system alongside the site, so the client gets both the storefront and the keys.

steps.title: QA, Security & Handoff
steps.description: Run automated security checks, test the CMS interface, verify responsive behavior. The client gets a web-based editor (similar to WordPress) where they can log in, edit content, and manage their site — no code required. Everything is open source and fully owned.

opportunity.label: The Opportunity
opportunity.heading: Where Workhorse + GoDoGood fit
paragraphs: GoDoGood has the client relationships, the brand strategy expertise, and the nonprofit market knowledge. Workhorse has the design eye and the agentic development capability. Together, the model looks like this:

GoDoGood handles brand strategy, client relationships, and ongoing retainer work (campaigns, social, email, impact reports). Workhorse handles UI/UX design and agentic development — turning brand direction into a live, open-source website in days instead of months.

The entry point is the $7,500–$15,000 website + brand refresh — the sweet spot where traditional agencies either turn clients away or stick them in a WordPress queue. With agentic development, this becomes a fast, high-quality, profitable engagement that opens the door to ongoing work.

stack.label: Under the Hood
stack.heading: The technology stack
stack.intro: For the technically curious — this is what's powering agentic web design today.

cards.title: Claude Code
cards.description: Anthropic's AI coding agent. Writes production-quality front-end and back-end code from natural language prompts. Spawns multiple agents for parallel development.

cards.title: Sanity (Headless CMS)
cards.description: A modern, structured content management system. Provides a clean editor interface for clients — similar to WordPress admin, but built specifically for the content on their site.

cards.title: Next.js / React
cards.description: The front-end framework. Industry standard, open source, used by the biggest companies in the world. Fast, accessible, and fully owned by the client.

cards.title: GitHub + Standard Hosting
cards.description: Code lives in a repository the client owns. Hosted on the same infrastructure as any WordPress site — Vercel, Netlify, or a traditional server. Nothing exotic.
